mn121:1.2: at one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in the Eastern Park, in the palace of Migāra's mother.
mn121:10.1: Again, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, not attending to the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññaṁ*], not attending to the *saññā* of the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasaññaṁ*], attends to singleness dependent on the *nimitta*-less *samādhi* of mind [*animittaṁ cetosamādhiṁ*].
mn121:10.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that *nimitta*-less *samādhi* of mind [*animitte cetosamādhimhi*], becomes clear, settles, and is resolved upon it.
mn121:10.4: 'Whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññaṁ*] are not present here; whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasaññaṁ*] are not present here. There is only this measure of disturbance, namely—
mn121:10.5: this very body, dependent on the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanikaṁ*], conditioned by life.'
mn121:10.6: He understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññāya*]'; he understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasaññāyā*]'; he understands: 'There is only this non-emptiness, namely—
mn121:10.7: this very body, dependent on the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanikaṁ*], conditioned by life.'
mn121:10.8: Thus, whatever is not there, he observes it as empty of that; but whatever remains there, he understands: 'This, being present, exists.'
mn121:10.9: In this way, Ānanda, this too becomes for him an entry into emptiness that is factual, not mistaken, and purified.
mn121:11.1: Again, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, not attending to the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññaṁ*], not attending to the *saññā* of the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasaññaṁ*], attends to singleness dependent on the *nimitta*-less *samādhi* of mind [*animittaṁ cetosamādhiṁ*].
mn121:11.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that *nimitta*-less *samādhi* of mind [*animitte cetosamādhimhi*], becomes clear, settles, and is resolved upon it.
mn121:11.4: 'This *nimitta*-less *samādhi* of mind [*animitto cetosamādhi*] too is constructed and volitionally intended.'
mn121:11.5: He understands: 'But whatever is constructed and volitionally intended is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], subject to cessation by nature.'
mn121:11.6: For him, knowing thus, seeing thus, the mind [*cittaṁ*] is liberated from the *āsava* of sensuality, the mind [*cittaṁ*] is liberated from the *āsava* of *bhava*, the mind [*cittaṁ*] is liberated from the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjā*].
mn121:11.7: When liberated, there is the knowledge: 'It is liberated.'
mn121:11.8: He understands: 'Birth is destroyed; the holy life has been lived; what had to be done has been done; there is no more of this state.'
mn121:12.2: 'Whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *āsava* of sensuality are not present here; whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *āsava* of *bhava* are not present here; whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjā*] are not present here. There is only this measure of disturbance, namely—
mn121:12.3: this very body, dependent on the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanikaṁ*], conditioned by life.'
mn121:12.4: He understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *āsava* of sensuality'; he understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *āsava* of *bhava*'; he understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjā*]'; he understands: 'There is only this non-emptiness, namely—
mn121:12.5: this very body, dependent on the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanikaṁ*], conditioned by life.'
mn121:12.6: Thus, whatever is not there, he observes it as empty of that; but whatever remains there, he understands: 'This, being present, exists.'
mn121:12.7: In this way, Ānanda, this becomes for him the supreme, unsurpassed entry into emptiness that is factual, not mistaken, and purified.
mn121:13.1: For whatever ascetics or brahmins in the past entered and dwelt in purified, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness, all of them entered and dwelt in this very purified, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness.
mn121:13.2: For whatever ascetics or brahmins in the future will enter and dwell in purified, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness, all of them will enter and dwell in this very purified, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness.
mn121:13.3: For whatever ascetics or brahmins at present enter and dwell in purified, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness, all of them enter and dwell in this very purified, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness.
mn121:13.4: Therefore, Ānanda, you should train thus: 'We will enter and dwell in purified, supreme, unsurpassed emptiness.'
mn121:13.7: Satisfied, Venerable Ānanda rejoiced in the *Bhagavā*'s statement.
mn121:13.8: The Shorter Emptiness is finished, the first.
mn121:2.1: Then Venerable Ānanda, rising from retreat in the evening, went to the *Bhagavā*; having gone to him, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat to one side. Sitting to one side, Venerable Ānanda said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn121:3.1: "At one time, *bhante*, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Sakyans, in a Sakyan town named Nagaraka.
mn121:3.2: There, *bhante*, I heard this in the presence of the *Bhagavā*, received it in his presence:
mn121:3.3: 'Ānanda, these days I dwell much in the dwelling of emptiness.'
mn121:3.4: Have I heard this well, *bhante*, grasped it well, attended to it well, borne it well in mind?"
mn121:3.5: "Indeed, Ānanda, you have heard this well, grasped it well, attended to it well, borne it well in mind.
mn121:3.6: Formerly, Ānanda, and now too, I dwell much in the dwelling of emptiness.
mn121:4.1: Just as, Ānanda, this palace of Migāra's mother is empty of elephants, cattle, horses, and mares, empty of gold and silver, empty of assemblies of women and men, and there is only this non-emptiness, namely—
mn121:4.10: Thus, whatever is not there, he observes it as empty of that; but whatever remains there, he understands: 'This, being present, exists.'
mn121:4.11: In this way, Ānanda, this too becomes for him an entry into emptiness that is factual, not mistaken, and purified.
mn121:4.3: so too, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, not attending to the *saññā* of village, not attending to the *saññā* of people, attends to singleness dependent on the *saññā* of wilderness.
mn121:4.4: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that *saññā* of wilderness, becomes clear, settles, and is resolved upon it.
mn121:4.6: 'Whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of village are not present here; whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of people are not present here. There is only this measure of disturbance, namely—
mn121:4.7: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of wilderness.'
mn121:4.8: He understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of village'; he understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of people'; he understands: 'There is only this non-emptiness, namely—
mn121:4.9: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of wilderness.'
mn121:5.1: Again, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, not attending to the *saññā* of people, not attending to the *saññā* of wilderness, attends to singleness dependent on the *saññā* of earth.
mn121:5.10: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of earth.'
mn121:5.11: Thus, whatever is not there, he observes it as empty of that; but whatever remains there, he understands: 'This, being present, exists.'
mn121:5.12: In this way, Ānanda, this too becomes for him an entry into emptiness that is factual, not mistaken, and purified.
mn121:5.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that *saññā* of earth, becomes clear, settles, and is resolved upon it.
mn121:5.3: Just as, Ānanda, an oxhide, well stretched with a hundred pegs, becomes free of wrinkles;
mn121:5.4: so too, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, not attending to all in this earth that is high and low, river ravines, places of stumps and thorns, and uneven mountains, attends to singleness dependent on the *saññā* of earth.
mn121:5.5: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that *saññā* of earth, becomes clear, settles, and is resolved upon it.
mn121:5.7: 'Whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of people are not present here; whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of wilderness are not present here. There is only this measure of disturbance, namely—
mn121:5.8: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of earth.'
mn121:5.9: He understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of people'; he understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of wilderness'; he understands: 'There is only this non-emptiness, namely—
mn121:6.1: Again, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, not attending to the *saññā* of wilderness, not attending to the *saññā* of earth, attends to singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññaṁ*].
mn121:6.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññāya*], becomes clear, settles, and is resolved upon it.
mn121:6.4: 'Whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of wilderness are not present here; whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of earth are not present here. There is only this measure of disturbance, namely—
mn121:6.5: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññaṁ*].'
mn121:6.6: He understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of wilderness'; he understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of earth'; he understands: 'There is only this non-emptiness, namely—
mn121:6.7: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññaṁ*].'
mn121:6.8: Thus, whatever is not there, he observes it as empty of that; but whatever remains there, he understands: 'This, being present, exists.'
mn121:6.9: In this way, Ānanda, this too becomes for him an entry into emptiness that is factual, not mistaken, and purified.
mn121:7.1: Again, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, not attending to the *saññā* of earth, not attending to the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññaṁ*], attends to singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññaṁ*].
mn121:7.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññāya*], becomes clear, settles, and is resolved upon it.
mn121:7.4: 'Whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of earth are not present here; whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññaṁ*] are not present here. There is only this measure of disturbance, namely—
mn121:7.5: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññaṁ*].'
mn121:7.6: He understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of earth'; he understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññāya*]'; he understands: 'There is only this non-emptiness, namely—
mn121:7.7: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññaṁ*].'
mn121:7.8: Thus, whatever is not there, he observes it as empty of that; but whatever remains there, he understands: 'This, being present, exists.'
mn121:7.9: In this way, Ānanda, this too becomes for him an entry into emptiness that is factual, not mistaken, and purified.
mn121:8.1: Again, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, not attending to the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññaṁ*], not attending to the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññaṁ*], attends to singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññaṁ*].
mn121:8.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññāya*], becomes clear, settles, and is resolved upon it.
mn121:8.4: 'Whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññaṁ*] are not present here; whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññaṁ*] are not present here. There is only this measure of disturbance, namely—
mn121:8.5: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññaṁ*].'
mn121:8.6: He understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanasaññāya*]'; he understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññāya*]'; he understands: 'There is only this non-emptiness, namely—
mn121:8.7: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññaṁ*].'
mn121:8.8: Thus, whatever is not there, he observes it as empty of that; but whatever remains there, he understands: 'This, being present, exists.'
mn121:8.9: In this way, Ānanda, this too becomes for him an entry into emptiness that is factual, not mistaken, and purified.
mn121:9.1: Again, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, not attending to the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññaṁ*], not attending to the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññaṁ*], attends to singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasaññaṁ*].
mn121:9.2: His mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into that *saññā* of the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasaññāya*], becomes clear, settles, and is resolved upon it.
mn121:9.4: 'Whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññaṁ*] are not present here; whatever disturbances there might be dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññaṁ*] are not present here. There is only this measure of disturbance, namely—
mn121:9.5: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasaññaṁ*].'
mn121:9.6: He understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanasaññāya*]'; he understands: 'This field of *saññā* is empty of the *saññā* of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanasaññāya*]'; he understands: 'There is only this non-emptiness, namely—
mn121:9.7: singleness dependent on the *saññā* of the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasaññaṁ*].'
mn121:9.8: Thus, whatever is not there, he observes it as empty of that; but whatever remains there, he understands: 'This, being present, exists.'
mn121:9.9: In this way, Ānanda, this too becomes for him an entry into emptiness that is factual, not mistaken, and purified.
